summary: Gruber reviews Viber, a free VOIP app for iPhone that uses phone numbers as identifiers and works over both Wi-Fi and 3G. He praises its easy setup and superior audio quality compared to actual AT&T phone calls, but criticizes its poor phone number matching, unsolicited push notifications to contacts, and inability to fully replace the built-in Phone app. His central concern is Viber's lack of any revenue model — calls are free, there are no ads, and the company admits they haven't figured out how to make money. He contrasts this with companies like Google and Instagram, where revenue paths were always obvious even before they were implemented.
key_points: Viber's VOIP call quality over AT&T 3G is far superior to actual cellular voice calls, which Gruber calls shameful
The app sends push notifications to your contacts when you sign up without your consent, which Gruber opposes on principle
Viber aspires to replace the built-in Phone app but falls short — no voicemail or recent calls access
The company has no revenue model and no plan for one, which Gruber finds deeply suspect
Unlike Viber, companies like Google and Instagram always had obvious paths to monetization even when they launched for free
money_quotes: The poor audio quality of iPhone voice calls in the U.S. is shameful.
I'm opposed to any service that sends notifications to others on my behalf without my consent.
Color me old-fashioned, but I'm skeptical of any company that comes out of the gate with a product that generates no revenue whatsoever.
If you get really good at spending money, you're going to be really good at spending money. If you have to work on making money from day one, you're going to get really damn good at making money.
If you're not practicing making money, you're not going to be able to flip that switch and just know how to do it really well.
With Viber, I'm not sure they have an obvious revenue route if all calls are already free of charge. Charge for voice mail?
key_insight: A free product with no revenue plan and no obvious path to one is fundamentally different from a free product where monetization is simply deferred — the former is a red flag, not a strategy.
